title: Fix deprecated bd sync references and update beads docs post-upgrade
description:
## Goal / Problem

After upgrading beads from v0.49.6 to v0.55.4 (Dolt backend), multiple docs still reference `bd sync` which is deprecated for Dolt. The `.beads/README.md` is generic boilerplate. `AGENTS.md` Landing the Plane protocol is outdated. Label commands missing from quick references.

## Background / Context

- Beads v0.55.4 uses embedded Dolt — no daemon, no `bd sync`
- Git hooks (pre-commit/post-merge/pre-push/post-checkout) handle Dolt-to-JSONL sync automatically
- For manual sync: `bd export` then git commit
- Full reference: `docs/reference/beads-knowledge.md`
- **Pattern reference:** existing `docs/reference/beads-knowledge.md` for correct terminology

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Project tooling / developer workflow (not domain code) |
| Ubiquitous Language | N/A — docs-only change |
| Layer | N/A — no src/ changes |

## Design

No code changes. Documentation-only updates across multiple files.

## Steps

1. Replace `bd sync` in `CLAUDE.md:34` quick reference with comment explaining hooks handle sync automatically.
2. Replace `bd sync` in `.claude/CLAUDE.md:26` same way.
3. Update `AGENTS.md` lines 12 and 27 — replace `bd sync` in quick reference and Landing the Plane workflow.
4. Update `.claude/agents/project-manager.md` lines 50 and 65 — session handoff and commands reference.
5. Rewrite `.beads/README.md` to reflect actual Dolt-based setup (not generic marketing boilerplate).
6. Add `bd label add/remove` to CLAUDE.md quick reference sections (used heavily for review_needed workflow).
7. Add note about molecules/wisps as advanced features in beads-knowledge.md cross-reference.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Zero occurrences of `bd sync` in non-reference docs (beads-knowledge.md can keep it for documentation purposes)
- [ ] `.beads/README.md` reflects Dolt backend, hooks-based sync, our actual workflow
- [ ] `AGENTS.md` Landing the Plane uses correct post-Dolt workflow
- [ ] Label commands visible in CLAUDE.md quick reference
- [ ] All quality gates pass after changes

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| Re-init overwrites README | `bd init` run again | Custom `.beads/README.md` may be lost |
| Factory reset overwrites AGENTS | `bd setup factory` | Custom `AGENTS.md` changes may be lost |

## Quality Gates

```bash
uv run ruff check .
uv run mypy .
uv run pytest
```

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass

## Risks / Dependencies

- `.beads/README.md` is generated by `bd init` — our custom version may get overwritten on re-init
- `AGENTS.md` is also managed by beads (`bd setup factory`) — custom changes may get overwritten

## Commit Message Format

```
docs: update beads references for v0.55.4 Dolt backend
```

title: Rename Antigravity to Roo Code across project
description:
## Goal / Problem

The k7m.1 research spike confirmed that "Antigravity" was the original project name, now primarily known as "Roo Code". All references throughout the codebase need updating to use the current name.

## Background / Context

- k7m.1 research report (`docs/research/20260222_knowledge_base_structure.md`) section 1.3 documents the rename
- Roo Code uses `.roo/` directory with `rules-*` mode files (not `.antigravity/rules.md`)
- Global config: `~/.roo/` (not `~/.antigravity/`)
- The rename affects product docs, PRD, DDD, shell script, README, and beads tickets
- **Pattern reference:** Research report preserves historical context and should NOT be modified

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Tool Translation (Supporting) |
| Ubiquitous Language | "Roo Code", "mode files", "rules-*" |
| Layer | Infrastructure (tool config generation) |

## Steps

1. Update `CLAUDE.md:7` — project overview tool list reference from Antigravity to Roo Code
2. Update `README.md:11, 46` — description and comparison table entries
3. Update `bin/alty` — shell script references:
   - `.antigravity/` → `.roo/`
   - `rules.md` → `rules-*` mode files
   - `setup_antigravity()` → `setup_roo_code()`
4. Update `docs/PRD.md` — 7+ references including directory structure, tool matrix, capabilities
5. Update `docs/DDD.md:165` — tool translation subdomain reference
6. Update `docs/reference/project_setup_reference.sh:7` — comment
7. Update beads tickets that reference Antigravity (k7m.16, k7m.21, others in JSONL)
8. Do NOT update research reports (they document historical findings)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Zero occurrences of "Antigravity" in non-research docs (research reports preserve historical context)
- [ ] `bin/alty` uses `.roo/` directory and `rules-*` mode file pattern
- [ ] PRD tool matrix shows "Roo Code" with correct config format
- [ ] DDD.md tool translation shows Roo Code
- [ ] Shell script function renamed and tested
- [ ] All quality gates pass

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| Research reports | Files in `docs/research/` | Left unchanged (historical context) |
| Case sensitivity | "antigravity" vs "Antigravity" | Both forms updated in non-research files |
| Already-updated tickets | k7m.16, k7m.21 | Verify correct, no double-update |

## Quality Gates

```bash
uv run ruff check .
uv run mypy .
uv run pytest
```

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Manual verification: `grep -ri antigravity` shows only research report hits

## Risks / Dependencies

- `bin/alty` shell script changes need manual testing (no automated tests yet)
- Roo Code config format may need a spike if `.roo/rules-*` pattern is more complex than `.antigravity/rules.md`
- Some beads tickets already updated in previous session (k7m.16, k7m.21) — verify no double-update

## Commit Message Format

```
docs: rename Antigravity to Roo Code across project
```

title: Epic: alty Phase 1 — Foundation
description:
Bootstrap alty using its own process. Create PRD, DDD artifacts, Architecture, and identify spikes. No implementation until domain is understood.

## Success Metrics

title: Spike: Knowledge base structure for tool conventions
description:
## Research Question

How should we organize the knowledge base for AI coding tool conventions (Claude Code, Cursor, Antigravity, OpenCode) so docs are RLM-addressable, version-tracked (current + 3 previous major versions), and maintainable — including a foundation for drift detection?

## Timebox

**Timebox:** 4 hours

## Background / Context

- White-hacker agent uses RLM pattern: docs are addressable variables, not search targets
- Need to cover: config file formats, agent profile formats, command/rule formats, .gitignore patterns
- Must support 4 tools with version tracking
- Knowledge must be useful for both guided bootstrap AND ongoing development
- **Must include global config paths** — each tool has global settings (e.g. ~/.claude/) that override local project settings. Knowledge base must document these paths per tool per version so alty init can detect conflicts.
- **Tool translation is P0** (moved from P1 per k7m.9 research and PRD update). This spike directly feeds the P0 multi-tool config generation feature. The KB structure must support generation of domain-aware configs, not just reference docs.
- **Drift detection is P1** — The KB must be structured so that a future drift detection system can compare stored conventions against actual tool versions. Design the schema with this in mind even though detection logic is P1.
- **k7m.4 spike outputs** — CLI design defined KnowledgeLookupPort (application port), MCP resources alty://knowledge/*, and .alty/knowledge/ directory. This spike must align with those interfaces.
- Links: docs/DDD.md §Knowledge Base context, docs/research/20260222_cli_mcp_design.md §Knowledge Base

## Investigation Approach

1. Document each tool's config structure (files, formats, capabilities)
2. **Document global vs local config precedence per tool**
3. **Map global config paths** (e.g. ~/.claude/, ~/.cursor/) per OS
4. Identify commonalities and differences across tools
5. Design directory structure that supports RLM lookup
6. Design versioning scheme (how to track current + 3 prev)
7. **Design drift-detection-ready schema** — what metadata per convention entry enables future staleness checks (version ranges, changelog URLs, deprecation flags)?

## Expected Deliverables

- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_knowledge_base_structure.md
- [ ] Proposed directory structure for .alty/knowledge/
- [ ] RLM lookup table design
- [ ] **Global config path registry per tool** (path, what it controls, override behavior)
- [ ] Version management strategy
- [ ] **Drift detection schema design** (metadata fields that enable future version comparison)
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Research question answered with clear recommendation for KB structure
- [ ] Findings documented in research report
- [ ] Directory structure aligns with KnowledgeLookupPort from k7m.4
- [ ] Schema supports RLM addressing (O(1) lookup by tool+version+topic)
- [ ] Drift detection metadata fields defined (even though detection logic is P1)
- [ ] Follow-up implementation tickets created (if applicable)
- [ ] Timebox respected (4 hours, or extended with justification)

title: Spike: Architecture fitness function generation design
description:
## Research Question

How should alty map a bounded context map to executable architecture fitness function tests (import-linter TOML contracts + pytestarch test files)?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background
Competitive research (k7m.9) identified Architecture Fitness Functions as a P0 launch feature -- the core technical moat. Research into Python architecture testing tools found:

- **import-linter** (v2.10, BSD-2) -- config-file based (TOML contracts in pyproject.toml). Contract types: layers, forbidden, independence, acyclic_siblings, protected, custom. Underlying engine: grimp (programmatic import graph API).
- **pytestarch** (v4.0.1, Apache-2.0) -- pure Python fluent API, integrates with pytest. Best candidate for programmatic test file generation.
- **deply** (v0.8.0, BSD-3) -- YAML-driven, also enforces naming conventions and generates Mermaid diagrams.

**Key finding:** Java has Context Mapper + ArchUnit + jMolecules 2.0 + Spring Modulith for DDD-to-architecture-test pipelines. Python has NO equivalent. alty would be building novel capability.

**Risk:** import-linter Python API is intentionally minimal (read-only config). Generating contracts means generating TOML programmatically -- needs a Pydantic model layer to avoid string-manipulation bugs.

## Research Input: k7m.2 Treatment Levels per Classification
The k7m.2 consolidated research (docs/research/20260222_guided_ddd_question_framework_consolidated.md, Section 3) defines treatment levels that drive contract strictness:
- **Core**: layers + forbidden + independence + acyclic_siblings contracts (strict)
- **Supporting**: layers + basic forbidden contracts (moderate)
- **Generic**: single forbidden contract — ACL boundary only (minimal)

The generation pipeline must read the complexity budget YAML output and select contract types accordingly.

## Research Input: k7m.5 DDD Artifacts
The DDD (docs/DDD.md) defines the concrete input for this spike:
- **Bounded context names**: Guided Discovery, Domain Model, Architecture Testing, Ticket Pipeline, Ticket Freshness, Tool Translation, Knowledge Base, Bootstrap, Rescue
- **FitnessTestSuite aggregate** (Architecture Testing context) with 5 invariants:
  1. Every bounded context must have at least one contract
  2. Core subdomains must have all 4 contract types (layers, forbidden, independence, acyclic_siblings)
  3. Supporting must have at least layers + forbidden
  4. Generic must have at least one forbidden (ACL boundary)
  5. No contract may reference a module outside its bounded context except through defined relationships
- **Context map** defines the integration patterns (domain events, queries, ACLs) that contracts must enforce
- Use these as the concrete bounded context map schema rather than designing one from scratch

## Investigation Steps
1. Design the bounded context map schema (YAML/TOML) -- minimal input that captures enough to emit contracts
2. Map DDD concepts to import-linter contract types:
   - Bounded context boundaries -> layer contracts
   - Dependency direction rules -> forbidden import contracts
   - Aggregate isolation -> independence contracts
3. Map DDD concepts to pytestarch rules:
   - Domain layer purity -> no-import rules
   - Port/adapter separation -> naming + import rules
4. Design the generation pipeline: bounded context map -> intermediate model -> test files
5. Evaluate: import-linter TOML vs pytestarch .py files vs both
6. Prototype: generate sample contracts from a sample bounded context map

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_fitness_function_design.md
- [ ] Bounded context map input schema (minimal effective format)
- [ ] DDD concept -> architecture test mapping table
- [ ] Generation pipeline design (input -> intermediate model -> output files)
- [ ] ADR: import-linter vs pytestarch vs hybrid approach
- [ ] Sample: bounded context map + generated test files
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Domain story to ticket pipeline design
description:
## Research Question

How should alty auto-generate dependency-ordered beads tickets from DDD artifacts (domain stories, bounded contexts, aggregates)?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background
Competitive research (k7m.9) identified Domain Story to Ticket Pipeline as a P0 launch feature. This is what makes alty more than a doc generator -- it produces actionable, ordered work items.

The pipeline should:
- Read DDD artifacts (bounded contexts, aggregates, domain events)
- Generate beads epics per bounded context
- Generate tasks ordered by aggregate dependency (foundation first)
- Pre-fill each ticket with TDD red/green/refactor phases
- Map SOLID principles per ticket
- Derive acceptance criteria from domain invariants
- Set blocking relationships from the context map

## Research Input: k7m.2 Complexity Budget Output
The k7m.2 consolidated research (docs/research/20260222_guided_ddd_question_framework_consolidated.md, Section 3) defines complexity budget YAML output that drives ticket detail levels:
- **Core**: Full AC, TDD phases, SOLID mapping, edge cases, >= 90% domain coverage
- **Supporting**: Standard AC, basic tests, >= 80% coverage
- **Generic**: Stub tickets ("integrate X"), verify boundary, >= 60% coverage

The ticket pipeline must read the complexity budget classification and generate appropriately detailed tickets per subdomain.

## Research Input: PRD Traceability via RLM Pattern (discovered during dogfooding)
During development, we found that ripple review catches *freshness* ("did a dependency change?") but NOT *completeness* ("does this ticket cover all PRD capabilities it should?"). A ticket (k7m.4) was missing P0 PRD items (doc-health commands) that were never added — no ripple would have caught this because it was missing from the start.

**Solution implemented for alty's own workflow:**  — an RLM-addressable command that maps every PRD P0/P1 capability to its bounded context and expected ticket scope. The grooming checklist step 2 now runs  to detect gaps.

**The ticket pipeline must generate this for users too:**
- **Generate a  command** as part of the seeded project, with:
  - A capability map derived from the generated PRD (each P0/P1 item gets an ID)
  - Bounded context mapping from the generated DDD artifacts
  - Expected ticket scope from the generated ticket plan
- **After generating all tickets, run the traceability check** — report any PRD capabilities not covered by any ticket (the "coverage gap" report)
- **Include PRD traceability as a grooming checklist step** in generated CLAUDE.md files
- **Two complementary safety nets for ticket health:**
  -  (RLM pattern) — catches *completeness gaps* ("was a PRD capability never assigned?")
  - Usage: bd-ripple <closed-ticket-id> [context summary]

Flags open siblings and dependents with review_needed after a ticket closes. (event-driven) — catches *freshness decay* ("did a dependency change the context?")
  - Both follow the same principle: documents/tickets as addressable variables, not search targets

## Research Input: k7m.5 DDD Artifacts
The DDD (docs/DDD.md) defines the concrete input for this spike:
- **TicketPlan aggregate** (Ticket Pipeline context) with 5 invariants:
  1. Every bounded context must produce exactly one epic
  2. Foundation aggregates (no dependencies) must be ordered first
  3. No circular dependencies in the generated ticket graph
  4. Core subdomain tickets must have full AC, TDD phases, and SOLID mapping
  5. No ticket may reference an aggregate from a different bounded context without a defined relationship
- **TicketDetailLevel value object**: full (Core), standard (Supporting), stub (Generic)
- **DomainModel aggregate** structure defines the input format — use docs/DDD.md sections as the machine-readable schema rather than designing a new one
- **DependencyOrder**: context map relationships define the build sequence

## Investigation Steps
1. Define the DDD artifact input format that feeds the pipeline
   - What minimum structure from docs/DDD.md is machine-readable?
   - YAML front matter? Structured markdown sections? Separate YAML file?
2. Design the ticket generation algorithm
   - How to determine dependency order from aggregate relationships?
   - How to split work into right-sized tickets (not too coarse, not too fine)?
   - How to detect which aggregates are foundational (no dependencies)?
3. Design ticket content templates
   - TDD phase mapping: which domain concept maps to which test type?
   - SOLID mapping: which principle applies to which ticket?
   - Acceptance criteria: how to derive from domain invariants?
4. Design the beads integration
   - Use bd create programmatically or generate JSONL directly?
   - How to set parent/child and dependency relationships?
5. Evaluate: fully automated vs human-in-the-loop (preview + confirm)

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ticket_pipeline_design.md
- [ ] DDD artifact input format specification
- [ ] Ticket generation algorithm design (ordering, sizing, dependencies)
- [ ] Ticket content template (TDD phases, SOLID mapping, acceptance criteria)
- [ ] Beads integration approach (programmatic vs JSONL)
- [ ] Sample: DDD artifacts -> generated ticket set
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation
notes:
**DOGFOODING INSIGHT (2026-02-23):** We discovered that all 27 dependencies in the k7m epic were described in ticket text but never formally set with `bd dep add`. This made `bd blocked` return empty and `bd ready` return everything — agents could pick up tickets with unmet prerequisites. Ripple review would also fail since it traverses the formal dependency graph.

**REQUIREMENT for ticket pipeline design:** The generated ticket pipeline MUST:
1. Call `bd dep add <ticket> <depends-on>` for every dependency, not just describe them in text
2. The BeadsWriterPort interface must include a `set_dependency(ticket_id, depends_on_id)` method
3. After writing all tickets, verify with `bd blocked` that the graph is correct
4. The DependencyOrder topological sort must produce both the ordering AND the formal dep-add commands

This is the difference between "documentation" and "enforcement" — text deps are documentation, formal deps are enforcement.

title: Spike: Ticket freshness and ripple review design
description:
## Research Question

How should alty implement event-driven ticket freshness detection (ripple review) so that completing a ticket automatically flags open dependents/siblings for re-review with a context diff?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background

We experienced this problem first-hand: closing k7m.9 (killer features spike) changed the context for k7m.2, k7m.3, k7m.4, and others. We had to manually groom each ticket to align with the research findings. No tool in the market (Jira, Linear, Kiro, GitHub stale-bot) does event-based staleness detection -- all use time-based inactivity only.

Research (docs/research/20260222_backlog_freshness_ticket_decay.md) identified four principles:
1. **Deferred detail** -- far-term tickets are stubs, detailed AC added just before work starts
2. **Event-triggered ripple flag** -- on ticket close, flag open dependents with review_needed
3. **Lightweight freshness metadata** -- review_needed + last_reviewed fields per ticket
4. **Context diff** -- record what the closed ticket produced so reviewers know what changed

Design decision (approved): **Approach 2 -- Ripple Flag + Context Diff** with human-in-the-loop. Agents see flags and present suggested updates to the user for approval. No automatic ticket rewrites.

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Freshness |
| Aggregates | RippleReview (4 invariants — see docs/DDD.md § 6) |
| Ubiquitous Language | ripple review, freshness, context rot, review_needed, last_reviewed, context diff, two-tier generation, after-close protocol |
| Layer | Application (orchestration of close -> flag -> review flow) + Infrastructure (beads integration) |

## Investigation Steps

1. **Design the ripple review data model**
   - What fields are added to beads ticket schema? (review_needed, last_reviewed, ripple_context)
   - How is ripple_context structured? (summary text? list of deliverables? link to research report?)
   - Should review_needed carry the ID of the ticket that triggered it?

2. **Design the close-time hook**
   - On bd close, how to traverse the dependency graph (depends_on, siblings in same epic)
   - Should it also flag tickets with related_to relationships?
   - How to capture the context summary -- manual input at close time? Auto-extract from ticket description/notes?
   - Output format: terminal message listing flagged tickets

3. **Design the pick-up-time flow**
   - When an agent claims a flagged ticket (bd update --status in_progress), what happens?
   - Agent reads ripple_context, reads the completed ticket's output, drafts suggested updates
   - Presents suggestions to user: update description? update AC? change priority? no change needed?
   - User approves/modifies/dismisses
   - Flag cleared, last_reviewed updated

4. **Design alty ticket-health command**
   - Read-only report: count of review_needed tickets, oldest last_reviewed dates
   - Per-epic breakdown
   - Integration with alty doc-health (unified health dashboard?)

5. **Design two-tier ticket generation**
   - How does the ticket pipeline (k7m.11) generate stubs vs full tickets?
   - What qualifies as "near-term" vs "far-term"? (distance from ready state? priority? epic phase?)
   - Stub format: title + one-sentence summary + dependencies + epic link, no detailed AC

6. **Design the after-close protocol**
   - The full after-close sequence: (1) ripple review flagged tickets → (2) fix flagged tickets → (3) groom next ready ticket (includes PRD traceability) → (4) report to user
   - Two complementary safety nets: ripple review catches *freshness decay*, PRD traceability catches *completeness gaps*
   - Agents must not start work on a review_needed ticket without presenting the review first
   - The generated CLAUDE.md must include this protocol so agents follow it automatically
   - Design the protocol as a CLAUDE.md section that alty generates into every bootstrapped project

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Research report exists at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ripple_review_design.md
- [ ] Ripple review data model defined (fields, schema, beads integration)
- [ ] Close-time hook designed with dependency traversal + context capture
- [ ] Pick-up-time flow designed with agent behavior + user approval UX
- [ ] alty ticket-health command designed (report format, per-epic breakdown)
- [ ] Two-tier generation rules defined (stub vs full ticket criteria)
- [ ] Updated beads ticket template includes freshness metadata fields
- [ ] **After-close protocol explicitly designed** — the 4-step sequence (ripple → fix flagged → groom next → report) is documented as a concrete specification that alty generates into CLAUDE.md of every bootstrapped project
- [ ] **After-close protocol includes PRD traceability** — grooming step must run `/prd-traceability` (or equivalent) to catch completeness gaps, not just freshness
- [ ] **After-close protocol is agent-enforceable** — design specifies that agents MUST run the protocol automatically after closing any ticket, without waiting for user to ask
- [ ] Follow-up implementation tickets created

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on beads supporting additional fields (review_needed, last_reviewed, ripple_context) -- may need beads schema extension or use of existing notes/metadata fields
- Depends on k7m.11 (ticket pipeline design) for two-tier generation integration
- Depends on prd-traceability command (.claude/commands/prd-traceability.md) for completeness checks
- Must not add bureaucratic overhead -- the process should cost < 2 minutes per ticket pick-up
notes: **PRD Traceability: C25** (Template-enforced ticket creation) — This spike's after-close protocol design must include template enforcement for follow-up tickets. See prd-traceability.md C25.

title: Set up Typer CLI entry point with subcommand stubs
description:
## Goal / Problem

Create the Typer CLI entry point for `vs` with all 15 subcommand stubs so that downstream tickets (k7m.15, k7m.16, k7m.17, k7m.22-k7m.26) can implement their command logic against a working CLI skeleton. Without this, no CLI command can be wired or tested end-to-end.

## Background / Context

- Spike k7m.4 selected Typer 0.24.1 as the CLI framework (ADR in `docs/research/20260222_cli_mcp_design.md`)
- CLI command tree defined in spike Section 2 with 15 commands mapped to bounded contexts
- Entry point: `vs = "src.infrastructure.cli.main:app"` (pyproject.toml `[project.scripts]`)
- Two subcommand groups: `generate` (4 subcommands) and `persona` (2 subcommands)
- Each stub command prints a "not yet implemented" message and exits cleanly
- **Pattern reference:** Spike Section 2 "CLI Entry Point" code snippet

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | CLI Framework (Generic subdomain) |
| Ubiquitous Language | N/A — infrastructure wiring only; no domain terms exposed |
| Layer | Infrastructure (`src/infrastructure/cli/`) |

Generic subdomain: CLI argument parsing is commodity (Typer handles it). Stub ticket detail follows the Generic treatment level — minimal domain logic, focus on wiring.

## Design

### Data Models

No domain models. This ticket creates infrastructure wiring only.

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `app` | Typer instance | Root CLI application |
| `generate_app` | Typer instance | Subcommand group for `alty generate *` |
| `persona_app` | Typer instance | Subcommand group for `alty persona *` |

### File Structure

```
src/
├── infrastructure/
│   └── cli/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── main.py          # app + subcommand groups + top-level commands
│       ├── generate.py      # generate subcommands (artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs)
│       └── persona.py       # persona subcommands (list, generate)
```

### Sequence / Flow

```
User                  Typer                    Stub Function
  |                     |                          |
  |-- alty init --------->|                          |
  |                     |-- route to init() ------>|
  |                     |                          |-- print "not implemented"
  |                     |<-- exit 0 ---------------|
  |<-- output --------- |
```

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | `main.py` only defines the app and wires subgroups; each command file owns its own stubs |
| **O**pen/Closed | New commands added by defining new functions — no modification to existing stubs |
| **L**iskov Substitution | N/A (no inheritance in stubs) |
| **I**nterface Segregation | Commands split across files by subcommand group (generate.py, persona.py) |
| **D**ependency Inversion | Stubs have no dependencies; when implemented, they will call application ports (Protocols) |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

Write failing tests that verify CLI commands exist and return exit code 0:

```python
# tests/infrastructure/cli/test_cli_entry_point.py
from typer.testing import CliRunner
from src.infrastructure.cli.main import app

runner = CliRunner()

def test_vs_help_shows_all_commands():
    """vs --help lists all 15 commands/groups."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["--help"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert "init" in result.output
    assert "guide" in result.output
    assert "generate" in result.output
    assert "detect" in result.output
    assert "check" in result.output
    assert "kb" in result.output
    assert "doc-health" in result.output
    assert "doc-review" in result.output
    assert "ticket-health" in result.output
    assert "persona" in result.output
    assert "version" in result.output

def test_vs_init_stub_exits_cleanly():
    """alty init prints not-implemented and exits 0."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["init"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0

def test_vs_generate_help_shows_subcommands():
    """alty generate --help lists artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["generate", "--help"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert "artifacts" in result.output
    assert "fitness" in result.output
    assert "tickets" in result.output
```
